ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Ajwani Malai Fish Tikka; mapped to closest parent fish dish category. Exact tandoor-marinated fish tikka term not present in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish, cooked — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for tandoor-grilled marinated fish preparation
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for fish tikka or tandoor-grilled marinated fish; A0CKP (cooked fish) used as the closest defensible parent anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for fish tikka preparation; not clinically coded as a distinct food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Ajwani Malai Fish Tikka is approximately 200 g, comprising 5 to 6 boneless fish pieces, providing roughly 380 kcal, 35 g protein, and 22 g fat per plate.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fish, tilapia, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175177) and Chicken tikka / tandoori fish analogues; supplemented by ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for spice-marinade adjustments
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Ajwani Malai Fish Tikka as a composite dish. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked white fish (tilapia, dry heat) as the protein/fat base, adjusted upward for cream (malai) and yoghurt marinade contribution to fat and saturated fat, and cross-checked against ICMR-NIN data for Indian fish preparations. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors (4/9/4 kcal per g protein/fat/carbohydrate). Sodium reflects marinade salt and spice blend typical of restaurant preparation. All values are estimates; direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish is not available in any approved source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Foods — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate based on yoghurt and spice marinade carbohydrate fraction
gi_source.source_match_notes: Ajwani Malai Fish Tikka contains only 4 g carbohydrate per 100 g, of which 1 g is sugar and 0.5 g is dietary fibre. The dish is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate grilled fish preparation. A GI of 35 is assigned as an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain yoghurt (GI ~35), which constitutes the primary carbohydrate-contributing ingredient in the marinade. Glycemic load per 200 g serving is calculated as GI (35) × available carbohydrate per serving (6.5 g) / 100 = 0.3, which is clinically negligible. The low GI reflects the protein-dominant, fat-present, low-carbohydrate nature of this dish.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 375 mg per 100 g (750 mg per standard 200 g serving) approaches one-third of the 2,000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should request reduced salt in the marinade to bring sodium below 300 mg per 100 g, and avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or salted chutneys to keep the total meal sodium within safe limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (4 g per 100 g) and high protein (17.5 g per 100 g) with minimal sugar (1 g per 100 g) produce a negligible glycemic load of 0.3 per serving, making this dish well-suited for blood glucose management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ajwani Malai Fish Tikka is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible glycemic load; however, sweetened chutneys or sauces served alongside should be avoided, and total caloric intake should be monitored given the cream-derived fat content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 4.5 g per 100 g (9 g per serving) and sodium at 375 mg per 100 g are the primary cardiovascular concerns; lean white fish provides beneficial protein without the saturated fat load of red meat.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request a yoghurt-only marinade (omitting or reducing fresh cream) to lower saturated fat below 3 g per 100 g, and limit portion size to 150 g to keep sodium intake within cardiovascular-safe thresholds.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, cinnamon, clove in garam masala) and lean fish protein support a low-inflammatory dietary profile, though nightshade-derived chilli may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory spice marinade; those sensitive to nightshades (chilli, pepper) should request a chilli-free preparation, and cream should be reduced to keep saturated fat below 3 g per 100 g.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ajwani Malai Fish Tikka is an Indian tandoor-grilled fish dish popular in Singapore's Indian restaurant and hawker scene. Boneless white fish fillets — typically dory or snapper — are cut into chunks and marinated in a creamy base of malai (fresh cream) and yoghurt, seasoned with ajwain (carom seeds), cumin, garam masala, ginger, garlic, green chilli, and lemon juice. The marinated fish is skewered and cooked in a high-heat tandoor oven, producing a lightly charred exterior with a moist, tender interior. The flavour profile is mildly spiced, aromatic, and creamy, with the distinctive anise-like note of ajwain complementing the richness of the malai marinade. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 190 kcal, 17.5 g protein, 11 g fat (4.5 g saturated), 4 g carbohydrate, and 375 mg sodium. The high protein and low carbohydrate content make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes and those following high-protein dietary patterns. The sodium level is moderate and warrants attention for hypertensive individuals. The cream marinade contributes saturated fat, which is a consideration for cardiovascular disease management. The dish is halal and gluten-free. It is typically served as a starter or main course with mint chutney, sliced onions, and lemon wedges. A standard restaurant portion is approximately 200 g (5–6 pieces), providing roughly 380 kcal and 35 g protein.

How many calories in fish tikka?

What spices are in malai fish tikka?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Ajwani Malai Fish Tikka as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked white fish (tilapia, dry heat) as the protein and fat base, adjusted for cream and yoghurt marinade contributions, and cross-checked against ICMR-NIN data for Indian fish preparations. The adaptation is methodologically sound but introduces estimation uncertainty, warranting a medium confidence rating.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked white fish (tilapia, FDC ID 175177) adjusted for malai (cream) and yoghurt marinade, cross-checked with ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017). Direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish is not available in any approved source. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific tandoor-grilled cream-marinated fish preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database as a composite dish entry as of 2026. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g (3.2) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.8) are estimated from USDA tilapia fatty acid profile adjusted for cream addition; these are approximate. 4. potassium_mg (340) is estimated from fish and yoghurt contribution per USDA FoodData Central; not directly measured for this dish. 5. Glycemic index is set to 35 (low category) as an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain yoghurt (GI ~35), which is the primary carbohydrate-contributing ingredient in the marinade. Glycemic load per 200 g serving is 0.3 (clinically negligible). 6. The input source listed (ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods) was used as a secondary cross-check for spice-marinade adjustments; USDA FoodData Central is the primary nutrient anchor. 7. Two portions in the input both have is_default: true; only one default portion (200 g plate) is carried forward. 8. ethnic_tags in the input ('Biryani', 'Butter Chicken', 'Naan') are dish names unrelated to this record and have been dropped from the output. 9. Aliases self-check: all strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array — confirmed consistent.
